items.correct: Meaning conveyed beyond what is literally said — generated when speakers obey or deliberately flout Grice's maxims. 'Some students passed' implicates 'not all', via the maxim of quantity.
items.misconception: Implicature is the same as the literal meaning of a sentence.
items.random_wrong: Implicature is a kind of grammatical mistake.
items.why: Implicature is precisely the gap between said and meant. Students conflate literal and intended meaning, missing why pragmatics is needed at all — much of communication is implied, not stated.

items.correct: Alternating between languages or varieties within a conversation or even a single utterance — often patterned by context, topic, identity or audience. Code-switching is rule-governed, not random.
items.misconception: Code-switching shows the speaker doesn't know either language properly.
items.random_wrong: Code-switching means using slang words in formal speech.
items.why: Code-switching requires fluency in both codes and follows linguistic constraints (e.g. avoiding ungrammatical splits). Students treat it as evidence of deficiency — research shows it is a sophisticated bilingual skill.

items.correct: A syntactic unit headed by a noun, which may include determiners, adjectives and modifiers — e.g. 'the big red car' is a noun phrase functioning as a single grammatical unit.
items.misconception: A noun phrase is just a noun by itself.
items.random_wrong: A noun phrase is a sentence that contains nouns.
items.why: Noun phrases can be one word ('cats') or many ('the cats that lived next door'). The phrase functions as a unit — subject, object or complement. Students reduce NP to a single noun, missing constituent structure.

items.q: Switching between language varieties is called.
items.why: It's Codeswitching. “Slang” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Fillers. “Tags” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many speech-feature types here (fillers, tag questions, discourse markers)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
